children.text: *	The present document (A/81/6 (Sect. 34)/Part A), consisting of the programme plan and programme performance information (part II; see General Assembly resolution 77/267, para. 10), will be replaced by a document containing parts A and B (A/81/6 (Sect. 34)) once part B is issued.

***	In keeping with paragraph 10 of General Assembly resolution 77/267, the part consisting of the programme plan and programme performance information (part II) is submitted through the Committee for Programme and Coordination for the consideration of the Assembly.

text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

34.1	The Department of Safety and Security is responsible for providing leadership, operational support and oversight of the United Nations security management system. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolution 59/276, by which the Department was established. The Department enables the United Nations to carry out its operations and programmes globally and provides support to approximately 180,000 personnel and 400,000 of their dependants in more than 125 States and territories.

34.2	The security environment in which the United Nations operates continues to be highly complex and unpredictable, driven by multiple factors, and is contributing to heightened risks for United Nations personnel and partners, including increasing incidents of arbitrary detention.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

34.3	The Department will continue to implement its activities and strengthen its capabilities in support of United Nations mandates and objectives through a risk-based approach tailored to the needs of United Nations system entities in carrying out their programmes while keeping their personnel safe in challenging operating environments. To sustain ongoing reforms for increased resilience, efficiency and results, the Department will continue to place specific emphasis on strategic coordination between subprogrammes and across United Nations Headquarters and field locations to enhance the coherence, timeliness and impact monitoring of the Department’s strategic initiatives.

34.4	The emphasis on resilience is driven by the risks that the operational environment poses to the Department’s ability to carry out its mission, as articulated in its enterprise risk management system. The Department will continue to strengthen its resilience through planning that is in line with United Nations programming priorities, a systematic focus on results-based management and the maintenance of robust coordination between the Department’s strategic capacities for preparedness and emergency response and field operations. The Department will also maintain its capacities and further identify partnerships to enhance its response to sudden, large-scale crises, drawing on rapidly deployable assets from across the Department’s varied sources of security expertise and in tandem with other United Nations entities for a cohesive response.

34.5	The Department will also continue to pursue operational efficiencies across its operations to meet increased demands within existing means, drawing on the findings of a self-evaluation of its digital transformation initiative, as explained in paragraph 34.18 below. This effort will focus on further digitizing processes, including analysis and reporting, and building digital literacy skills across the Department’s workforce. Efficiency will also remain a central dimension in the Department’s conduct of thematic evaluations, compliance monitoring and lessons learned exercises.

34.6	The Department will continue to implement the multi-year standardized access control project to upgrade the United Nations security infrastructure, as presented in the report of the Secretary-General on capital investment planning (A/79/510).

34.7	The Department will continue to update and upgrade its training programme in support of integrated security workforce performance in line with the evolving needs of United Nations programmes around the world. The Department’s advocacy and communication efforts will further stress, concretely and transparently, how security support contributes to the achievement of results by enabling the delivery of United Nations mandates. The Department will also continue to work towards reaching gender parity and pursue geographical representation and diversity in the profile of its workforce.

34.8	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, the Department will continue its commitment to close collaboration with implementing partners of the United Nations, including non-governmental organizations, notably through the Saving Lives Together framework, which promotes security-related information-sharing and best practice exchanges between the United Nations and international non-governmental organizations.

34.9	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Department will continue to play a leadership role, through the Inter-Agency Security Management Network, in the development, updating and implementation of system-wide security policies aimed at enabling United Nations operations within acceptable risk levels. In particular, the Department will continue to collaborate with the Network for an in-depth review of the United Nations security management system, initiated in 2025 at the request of the High-level Committee on Management of the United Nations System Chief Executives Board for Coordination (CEB). Furthermore, the Department will continue to participate in inter-agency coordination mechanisms, including the High-level Committee.

34.10	With regard to the external factors, the Department considered applicable risks identified in its risk register and, accordingly, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	Crises and security environments will continue to evolve quickly and unpredictably, and the ability of the United Nations to gain safe access to areas and communities for programme delivery will continue to be challenged. Simultaneous large-scale and complex emergencies will continue to require agility, flexibility and rapid security responses to support the scale-up, and adjustment as required, of the United Nations presence and humanitarian programmes;
(b)	Security needs will remain high during drawdowns and withdrawals of United Nations peace operations, and active engagement with Member States will be required when such transitions occur;

(c)	Personnel of the United Nations and its premises will continue to face direct and indirect threats, including from threat actors using innovative and advanced techniques, disinformation campaigns and misinformation, which will require new and specialized responses from the United Nations security management system;
(d)	Host Governments will continue to provide security support to the United Nations; and in some cases, this support may need to be supplemented depending on the security context and capacities;

(e)	Demand for enhanced levels of security risk analysis, training, advice, responses and solutions to support the security needs and obligations of United Nations Designated Security Officials and of members of the Security Management Team will continue to be high;
(f)	Adequate extrabudgetary resources are available for additional security capabilities to enable programming, notably in crisis situations.

34.11	The Department integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate, with an increased focus on equipping field operations with tools to promote a gender-sensitive approach to security management. On the basis of regular engagement with its network of focal points and in close collaboration with the Inter-Agency Security Management Network, it will also continue to refine its approach to creating a safe working environment for United Nations staff and operations and increasing awareness of and taking action to prevent sexual exploitation and abuse, among other matters.

34.12	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Department will engage with organizations of persons with disabilities to continue to build a supportive environment for persons with disabilities, including through the systematic inclusion of disability considerations in its risk management process, the regular review of premises accessibility for staff and delegates with disabilities and the monitoring of satisfaction with the level of support received.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

34.13	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In line with efforts towards organizational optimization, functions previously under subprogramme 3 related to the development of strategic partnerships with regional and global networks and to the development of system-wide security policy and guidance have been moved to the Office of the Under-Secretary-General. Subprogramme 3 has therefore been renamed “Specialized services” to reflect its refined focus, in line with its revised strategy and table of deliverables.

34.14	The Department will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.
Legislative mandates
34.15	The list below, which was reviewed in the preparation of the proposed programme budget, provides all mandates entrusted to the programme.
General Assembly resolutions and decisions

children.text: Evaluation activities
34.17	An evaluation of the Department’s digital transformation initiative, completed by the Department in 2025, has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

34.18	In response to the results of the evaluation referenced above, the Department will strengthen the monitoring of results achieved through various digital transformation initiatives, such as the modernization of the Department’s security incident recording system and the increased interoperability of personnel security information dissemination systems, and further identify opportunities to mainstream digital solutions across divisions and offices to maximize efficiency gains.
34.19	An evaluation of operational and strategic aspects of the Department’s risk management systems and procedures is planned for 2027.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Security and safety services
Objective

34.20	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure a safe and secure environment for all United Nations personnel, delegates, dignitaries and other visitors while conducting activities at United Nations Headquarters, offices away from Headquarters and regional commissions and while attending special events hosted by the United Nations at external locations.
Strategy

34.21	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will provide strategic coordination and management of the security and safety services at eight duty stations, as well as operational guidance and technical supervision with regard to security risk management, physical security, close protection, crisis management, fire safety, recruitment, resilience, training and oversight of security and safety projects at those locations.
34.22	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Safe, secure and efficient access of delegates, staff and visitors to United Nations premises and external special events;
(b)	Effective delivery of United Nations mandates and programmes at United Nations premises and external special events.
Programme performance in 2025
A safe and secure environment for all delegates and participants at the eightieth session of the General Assembly

34.23	The eightieth session of the General Assembly marked the Organization’s eightieth anniversary, and focused on renewing multilateralism under the theme “Better together: 80 years and more for peace, development and human rights”. This event saw an unprecedented level of security engagement and operational complexity at United Nations Headquarters. The subprogramme conducted an intensive planning exercise with host country law enforcement and emergency service partners and internal United Nations entities in preparing a safe and secure environment. Detailed risk assessments identified specific mitigation measures that were put in place to provide safety and security for the visiting Heads of State and Government and regular delegates. The subprogramme ensured seamless access control and safety procedures during the high-level week, and provided a safe and secure environment for all participants.
34.24	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 34.2).
Table 34.2
Performance measure

table_content.text: A total of 130 side events and 1,164 bilateral meetings conducted without any security incidents during the high-level week of the seventy-eighth session of the General Assembly
A total of 170 side events and 1,292 bilateral meetings conducted without any security incidents during the high-level week of the seventy-ninth session of the General Assembly
A total of 304 side events and 1,430 bilateral meetings conducted without any security incidents during the high-level week of the eightieth session of the General Assembly

Result 1: improved ease of movement of delegates and staff across duty stations – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

34.25	The subprogramme’s work contributed to new physical infrastructure in place in six duty stations to facilitate the ease of access of delegates and staff travelling between duty stations under a common platform for access control, which did not meet the planned target of physical infrastructure in place in eight duty stations. The target was not met due to additional technological requirements, which called for further upgrades for full implementation in the two remaining duty stations.

children.text: Result 3: improved safety and security decision-making – Proposed programme plan for 2027

34.29	Effective decision-making depends on timely, accurate and comparable data across all duty stations. The subprogramme currently compiles weekly, monthly, quarterly and annual Excel-based reports on security operations, activities and emerging trends and developments from all Division of Headquarters Security and Safety Services duty stations. Security managers in all duty stations use this data for better planning and coordination of access control for delegates, staff and visitors, to enhance preparedness and governance to safeguard headquarters premises, to strengthen security support for high-level meetings, conferences and large-scale events and to identify trends for security resourcing and planning for future delegate and visitor support.
Lessons learned and planned change

34.30	The lesson for the subprogramme was that manual spreadsheet reporting limits data quality, slows analysis and constrains the ability to generate real-time insights that are essential for safety and security. To achieve more reliable and actionable information, the subprogramme will transition to digitized data-collection models that feed directly into Power BI dashboards. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will design standardized digital reporting tools, automate data flows and strengthen analytics capacity to best assist timely decision-making by security managers in consultation with senior leadership. The digitized reporting mechanisms and Power BI dashboards will provide security managers and senior leadership with access to real-time information, thereby enhancing security planning and service delivery and improving access to Headquarters premises, optimizing support for meetings and events and providing clearer trend analysis that ultimately improves safety and security.
34.31	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 34.5).
Table 34.5
Performance measure

children.text: Deliverables
34.32	Table 34.6 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 34.6
Subprogramme 1: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Safety and security: security and access control for approximately 6 million people per year at United Nations Headquarters, offices away from Headquarters and regional economic and social commissions; security support for approximately 60 United Nations special events and conferences held away from United Nations premises; approximately 9,000 close protection operations for senior United Nations officials and visiting dignitaries; 2,600 security incident investigations; 2,400 fire safety checks.

Safety and security: security and access control for approximately 6 million people per year at United Nations Headquarters, offices away from Headquarters and regional economic and social commissions; security support for approximately 60 United Nations special events and conferences held away from United Nations premises; approximately 9,000 close protection operations for senior United Nations officials and visiting dignitaries; 2,600 security incident investigations; 2,400 fire safety checks.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Field operations
Objective

34.33	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the safety and security of personnel and the security of United Nations premises and assets in the United Nations field duty stations to enable the implementation of United Nations mandates, programmes and activities worldwide.
Strategy
34.34	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Provide operational security support for United Nations field programme delivery and field programme personnel through effective and timely security coordination and advice;

(b)	Provide consistent and integrated strategic and operational support to, and guidance for, field operations in the preparation for and management of security crises, including through the integration of core strategic capacities, specifically resilience and emergency response;
(c)	Deploy security professionals in a rapid and timely fashion to countries, missions and areas, including those affected by crisis situations and security contingencies;
(d)	Conduct security assessment missions to field locations to review and assess local security management processes and procedures;

(e)	Conduct regional workshops and global conferences for all of the Department’s senior field security professionals to share operational best practices and lessons learned and to ensure consistent security management practices and procedures;
(f)	Develop its capacity to capture and analyse data and deliver analytical products for security decision makers.
34.35	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved preparedness, effectiveness, efficiency, relevance and resilience of security risk management processes;
(b)	Enhanced capacity to enable United Nations programmes to be carried out in high-risk environments, in line with the United Nations goal to stay and deliver;
(c)	Enhanced ability of security decision makers to take risk-informed decisions;
(d)	Enhanced and streamlined security management measures in the field.
Programme performance in 2025
Increased operational efficiencies with a focus on high-risk locations

34.36	Faced with more unpredictable, complex and volatile security conditions in the field, compounded by the financial constraints, the subprogramme increased operational efficiencies and structural adjustments to maintain safety and security in the field and enable United Nations programme delivery. The subprogramme undertook various operational and structural changes to strengthen the efficiency and resilience of security services in field locations. The subprogramme redeployed security personnel to priority locations, shifted analytical resources closer to field operations and created additional multi-country offices to ensure coverage with much-reduced resources. Regional desk structures were reformed to create efficiencies and better serve field operations.
34.37	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 34.7).
Table 34.7
Performance measure

table_content.text: Highest-risk environments and essential security services in the field received concentrated operational support, safeguarding core mandate delivery and the continued operational delivery of United Nations programmes in the field. Structural changes also brought long-term efficiency, which will continue to mitigate the operational impact arising from financial pressures

Table 34.8
Performance measure

children.text: Result 3: risk-informed safety and security decision-making – Proposed programme plan for 2027

34.42	Security risk management is central to enabling United Nations security operations, as it provides a rigorous framework for the identification of security risks and mitigation measures and the residual risk after those mitigation measures have been implemented. This structured and standardized process allows security decision makers to analyse and manage safety and security risks in a consistent manner across diverse operational contexts. The subprogramme has worked on developing a quality assurance tool to identify where security risk management can be further improved and refined.
Lessons learned and planned change

34.43	The lesson for the subprogramme was that it was important to identify areas for improvement in the security risk management framework and the tool, based on inconsistencies and errors found through the collection and analysis of security risk management data. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will focus on improving the quality of security risk management outputs, simplifying the process and the tool and enhancing user experience and understanding, in order to make the security risk management framework not only easier for security decision makers and others to use but also even more impactful in identifying security threats to United Nations operations. Decision makers will be supported through targeted guidance and accompanying capacity-building activities.
34.44	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 34.10).

Performance measure

children.text: Deliverables
34.45	Table 34.11 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 34.11
Subprogramme 2: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

children.text: Subprogramme 3 – Specialized services
Objective

34.46	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the safety and security of United Nations operations, premises and personnel and to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of the security operations and security management system of the United Nations.
Strategy
34.47	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Implement the digital transformation strategy to provide the United Nations security management system with the operational backbone required for efficient, data-driven approaches to security risk management and to standardize and streamline business processes and technology for all aspects of security delivery, including personal protection services, emergency communications, critical incident stress management, physical security and road safety;
(b)	Provide leadership, coordination and oversight for safety and security training, including on topics such as resilience-building, security risk management and crisis management;
(c) 	Implement a new personal protection policy with the aim of improving standardization and consistency in the delivery of close protection for senior United Nations officials;

(d)	Provide leadership and coordination for a rapid psychosocial support response in the aftermath of critical incidents and collaborate with the United Nations system and external partners to build the psychological resilience of United Nations security management system personnel;
(e)	Lead the development of physical security guidance, standards and technical specifications and undertake physical security assessments of United Nations premises worldwide.
34.48	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Effective prevention of and coherent response to security incidents by United Nations security management system actors, through timely and efficient decision-making and enhanced access to data;
(b)	Effective management of risks for the movement of senior United Nations officials;
(c)	Mitigation of the short- and long-term psychological impact of exposure to critical incidents on United Nations security management system personnel;
(d)	Reduction of physical security risks to United Nations premises and personnel.
Programme performance in 2025
New digital systems strengthened the safety and security of United Nations personnel

34.49	The subprogramme strengthened its duty of care and operational security management functions through the delivery of integrated digital capabilities that enhanced accounting for personnel and situational awareness and decision-making across the United Nations security management system. The subprogramme worked towards deployment of the Department’s data platform and the updated version of the United Nations Security Management Information Network, establishing a modern foundation for secure data integration, analytics and information-sharing. These platforms enabled more consistent, accurate and timely access to operational data for security professionals at Headquarters and in the field.
34.50	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 34.12).
Table 34.12
Performance measure

table_content.text: The roll-out of the Department’s data platform and an enhanced United Nations Security Managers Information Network enabled security professionals in the United Nations to have access to more consistent, accurate and timely operational data, enabling improved security decision-making. The deployment of these tools also enhanced emergency communications in field operations and strengthened personnel accountability and crisis preparedness. It also improved integration and interoperability with Secretariat enterprise systems, allowing for better accounting for all United Nations personnel

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: consistent and coherent operations across the United Nations security management system – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

34.51	The subprogramme’s work contributed to all United Nations security management system security focal points and security decision makers being aware of and applying updated system policies and guidelines that took into account their operational realities, which met the planned target.
34.52	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see table 34.13).
Table 34.13
Performance measure

table_content.text: United Nations security management system security focal points and security decision makers had expanded access to and were informed of changes to system policies and guidelines
United Nations security management system security focal points and security decision makers identified and addressed emerging gaps in existing guidance

All United Nations security management system security focal points and security decision makers were aware of and applied updated system policies and guidelines that took into account their operational realities

All United Nations security management system security focal points and security decision makers are aware of and share operationally informed good practices and lessons on the application of policies and guidelines
The United Nations security management system policy framework reflects evolving operational requirements and facilitates the coherence of efforts

Standardized baseline for providing psychosocial approach is approved by the Inter-Agency Security Management Network and documented in the United Nations field manual on psychosocial support in crisis situations
United Nations counsellors were better informed about standardized psychosocial support during evacuations and relocations, applying specific field manual protocols system-wide

United Nations security management system organizations developed a shared understanding of a harmonized response to psychosocial support 
Families of over 250 humanitarian workers who lost their lives received standardized psychosocial support
Fifty psychosocial support providers certified in critical incident stress management, including in all field manual chapters

Improved operational efficiency by utilizing the harmonized approach to psychosocial support 
Uninterrupted delivery of critical care in high-risk duty stations through the implementation of joint activities (such as shared emergency helplines, joint training and stress counselling activities)

children.text: Result 2: strengthened personal protection services for senior United Nations officials – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

34.53	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 100 per cent of senior United Nations officials responding to the satisfaction survey expressing increased confidence in their personal protection, which exceeded the planned target of 25 per cent.
34.54	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 34.I).
Figure 34.I
Performance measure: percentage of senior United Nations officials expressing increased confidence in their personal protection (annual)
Result 3: harmonized approach to psychosocial support following critical incidents – Proposed programme plan for 2027

34.55	The subprogramme ensures that psychosocial support is embedded in the system-wide delivery of resilience-building and stress management training to personnel in high-risk duty stations, and in the provision of direct support to personnel and dependants following critical incidents. The subprogramme serves as the central body responsible for the timely coordination and the provision of psychosocial services within the United Nations security management system, ensuring inter-agency coordination on critical incident stress management and providing a rapid, professional response to critical incidents involving personnel.
Lessons learned and planned change

34.56	The lesson for the subprogramme was that well-coordinated psychosocial support during crisis situations is a strategic necessity for humanitarian and peacebuilding operations, particularly amid rising humanitarian worker casualties. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will continue to implement a standardized response – integrated directly into security emergency planning – which is vital for managing trauma from overlapping international crises. To fulfil its mandate, the subprogramme will continue global coordination meetings and regional cell meetings to maintain technical standards and policy alignment for the preparation for, response to and recovery from critical incidents and emergencies across United Nations security management system entities.
34.57	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 34.14).
Table 34.14

children.text: Deliverables
34.58	Table 34.15 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 34.15
Subprogramme 3: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Safety and security: emotional first aid and critical incident psychosocial support for United Nations security management system personnel; stress management training for personnel in field duty stations; technical supervision sessions for United Nations field counsellors; dedicated assessments of the physical security of United Nations premises globally; topical guidance, physical security training and analysis of data provided by the physical security assessment application; security training throughout the United Nations security management system; travel tracking and coordination of protective operations related to the movement of senior United Nations officials; planning and coordination of personal protection services with respective missions; mandatory certification training for close protection officers; training on digital tools, dashboard and site development and deployment services.
